summary: Third President of the United States (1801-1809), author of the Declaration of Independence, and architect of Monticello and the University of Virginia. Wrote Notes on the State of Virginia and a hand-cut, glue-pasted edition of the New Testament excising all miracles (The Life and Morals of Jesus of Nazareth). Called for a "wall of separation between church and state" in his 1802 letter to the Danbury Baptists. As president, fought the First Barbary War, signed the Embargo Act, secured the Louisiana Purchase, and commissioned the Lewis and Clark Expedition. Owned enslaved people at Monticello, including Sally Hemings, with whom James Callender alleged he had children. Reconciled and corresponded for fifteen years with his former rival John Adams; the two died within hours of each other on July 4, 1826.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: How Jefferson is identified in giveaway lines: third President of the United States and author/drafter of the Declaration of Independence, who earlier served as the first US Secretary of State under George Washington and as the second US Minister to France.

examples: This man introduced the word "pedicure" to English after his time in Paris, where he served as the second U.S. Minister to France

clues.clue: The only multi-term president with zero vetoes

works.name: Declaration of Independence & Political Philosophy
works.description: As part of the Committee of Five, Jefferson wrote the first draft of the Declaration of Independence, later debated and edited by Congress. His political thought distinguished a "natural aristocracy" of virtue and talent from an "artificial aristocracy" of wealth and birth, and championed an agrarian republic of yeoman farmers against Alexander Hamilton's centralized, pro-manufacturing vision. After resigning the vice presidency in protest of the Alien and Sedition Acts, he authored the Kentucky Resolutions, arguing that states could refuse to follow federal laws they deemed unconstitutional.

clues.clue: Wrote the first draft of the Declaration of Independence as part of the Committee of Five; Congress extensively debated the wording
examples: Congress extensively debated the wording of a text drafted by the Committee of Five whose first draft was written by this man

clues.clue: Paraphrased John Locke in articulating the "unalienable rights" of "life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness"
examples: name this American author who articulated the "unalienable rights" of "life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness" in The Declaration of Independence

clues.clue: Distinguished a "natural aristocracy" of "virtue and talents" from an "artificial aristocracy" of "wealth and birth"
examples: This man contrasted a "natural aristocracy" of "virtue and talents" with an "artificial aristocracy" of "wealth and birth"
This philosopher distinguished between a "natural aristocracy" based on virtue and talents and an artificial one based on wealth and birth

clues.clue: Advocated for an agrarian America of yeoman farmers, opposing Alexander Hamilton's centralized, pro-manufacturing vision
examples: Alexander Hamilton's pro-manufacturing outlook was opposed by what third American president?
Name this politician, who advocated for an America of yeoman farmers in contrast with the centralized, federal, and urban vision of Alexander Hamilton

clues.clue: His economic philosophy may have been inspired by the French Physiocrats; the American Communist Party founded a "School of Social Science" named for him
examples: The American Communist Party founded a "School of Social Science" named for this man, whose economic philosophy may have been inspired by French Physiocrats

clues.clue: Authored the Kentucky Resolutions, arguing states had a duty not to follow laws they felt were unconstitutional, after resigning the vice presidency in protest of the Alien and Sedition Acts
examples: name this author of the "Kentucky and Virginia Resolutions," the third president of the United States
This man argued that states had a duty not to follow laws they felt were unconstitutional after he resigned from the Vice-Presidency in protest of the Alien and Sedition Acts

clues.clue: Later described the Missouri Compromise as a "fire bell in the night" and warned that the "tree of liberty" must be refreshed with the blood of patriots and tyrants
examples: He described the Missouri Compromise as a "fire bell in the night" and said that the "tree of liberty" must be refreshed with the blood of patriots and tyrants

works.name: Religious Freedom & the "Wall of Separation"
works.description: Jefferson, author of the Virginia Statute for Religious Freedom, wrote to the Danbury Baptists of Connecticut in 1802 that the First Amendment erected "a wall of separation between church and state," a phrase later central to Establishment Clause jurisprudence. His dissatisfaction with religious institutions in Virginia led him to found the University of Virginia.

clues.clue: In an 1802 letter to the Danbury Baptists, called for a "wall of separation" between church and state
examples: In a letter to the Danbury Baptists, this philosopher called for a "Wall of Separation" between church and state
Defense of the Establishment Clause often cites this man's description of a "wall of separation between church and state" in a letter to Danbury Baptists

clues.clue: Author of the Virginia Statute for Religious Freedom
examples: "The wall of separation" is a phrase often attributed to this author of the Virginia Statute for Religious Freedom

clues.clue: Founded the University of Virginia, in Charlottesville, out of dissatisfaction with the state's religious institutions
examples: This man's dissatisfaction with religious institutions in his state led him to found a university in Charlottesville

works.name: The Jefferson Bible
works.description: Using a razor and glue, Jefferson cut and pasted passages from the New Testament to isolate "the very words only of Jesus," excising every miracle to produce a naturalized retelling of the Gospels, published under titles including The Philosophy of Jesus of Nazareth and The Life and Morals of Jesus of Nazareth.

clues.clue: Used a razor and glue to cut and paste sections of the New Testament, selecting only "the very words of Jesus" and excising every miracle
examples: Out of a desire to select "the very words only of Jesus," this person used a razor and glue to cut and paste sections of the New Testament
This author produced an edition of the New Testament that excised every miracle

clues.clue: Attempted to retell the Gospels without supernatural phenomena in a work titled "The Philosophy of Jesus of Nazareth"; the resulting text (also called The Life and Times of Jesus of Nazareth) consists solely of excised portions of the New Testament, reflecting deist/unitarian views
examples: This man attempted to retell the Gospels without supernatural phenomena in his work "The Philosophy of Jesus of Nazareth"
This philosopher's The Life and Times of Jesus of Nazareth solely consists of excised portions of the New Testament, reflecting his deist or unitarian views

clues.clue: Also wrote a Plan for Establishing Uniformity in the Coinage, Weights, and Measures of the United States, alongside his remixed Bible subtitled "The Life and Morals of Jesus of Nazareth"
examples: Identify this politician whose other writings include a Plan for Establishing Uniformity in the Coinage, Weights, and Measures of the United States and a remixed version of the Bible subtitled The Life and Morals of Jesus of Nazareth

works.name: Notes on the State of Virginia & Views on Race
works.description: Jefferson's only published book, broken into 23 "Queries" on subjects like climate and law, defended American nature against Comte de Buffon's theory that New World species (and people) were degenerate, including a table comparing quadruped sizes and a reproduced oration by the Mingo chief Logan. The book also contains Jefferson's ambivalent and often racist commentary on Black Americans, including his notorious criticism of the poet Phillis Wheatley.

clues.clue: Divided into 23 "Queries" (including "Climate" and "Laws") about his home state of Virginia
examples: A book by this author is divided into 23 "Queries," including "Climate," "Aborigenes," and "Laws"
His responses to 23 "queries" about his home state were published as Notes on the State of Virginia

clues.clue: Drew a table comparing quadruped sizes in Europe and North America to refute Comte de Buffon's theory that New World fauna were degenerate
examples: This author drew a table comparing the size of quadrupeds in Europe and North America to refute Buffon's theory that New World fauna were degenerate
Name this politician who also attacked Buffon's theory in Notes on the State of Virginia

clues.clue: Reproduced an oration by the Mingo chief Logan as part of his refutation of Buffon
examples: This writer reproduced an oration by the Mingo chief Logan as part of his refutation of Count de Buffon's theory that animals are smaller and people are stupider in America than in Europe

clues.clue: Wrote that Black Americans had "misery enough, God knows, but no poetry," criticizing the poet Phillis Wheatley
examples: This author wrote that Black Americans had "misery enough, God knows, but no poetry" in an infamous criticism of Phyllis Wheatley

clues.clue: In the "Manufactures" section, claimed "those who labor in the earth are the chosen people of God," idealizing agrarian "yeoman farmers"
examples: This politician claimed that "those who labor in the earth are the chosen people of God" in the "Manufactures" section of his "Notes on the State of Virginia." This man idealized "yeoman farmers"

clues.clue: Wrote that white women's ability to blush made them aesthetically superior to Black women
examples: In this person's "Notes on" his home state, he wrote that white women's ability to blush made them aesthetically superior to black women

clues.clue: In a discussed "query," contended that "Blacks are equal to Whites" and that slavery degrades both Black and white society (per Leo Marx's The Machine in the Garden)
examples: Leo Marx's book The Machine in the Garden theorizes that "frontier savagery" did not exist in this thinker's ideal republic and discusses a "query" from one of his works contending that "Blacks are equal to Whites," and that "slavery" is degrading for both "Black and White society"

works.name: Presidency: Barbary Wars, Embargo & Louisiana/Lewis and Clark
works.description: As president, Jefferson refused to continue paying tribute to the Pasha of Tripoli, sparking the First Barbary War, before ultimately negotiating a peace treaty; he later signed the Embargo Act. He secured the Louisiana Purchase and commissioned the Lewis and Clark Expedition, partly hoping it would discover a living mastodon after Jefferson himself had used mastodon fossils to rebut Buffon's degeneracy theory.

clues.clue: Refused to pay tribute to the Pasha of Tripoli (that his predecessor John Adams had negotiated), triggering the First Barbary War
examples: Name this chief executive whose decision not to pay tribute to North Africans led to the First Barbary War
This president started the war by refusing to pay Tripoli the tribute negotiated by his predecessor, John Adams

clues.clue: After a decisive victory over the Barbary Pirates, chose to sign a peace treaty rather than continue the war; also signed the Embargo Act
examples: After a decisive victory over the Barbary Pirates, this US president decided to sign a peace treaty with the them rather than continue the war to their destruction. This president also signed the Embargo Act
the president who signed the Embargo Act and drafted the Declaration of Independence

clues.clue: Commissioned/sponsored the Lewis and Clark Expedition (1803), partly hoping it would find a living mastodon
examples: This president hoped that his Lewis and Clark expedition would find a living mastodon

clues.clue: As a fossil collector, argued mastodon remains disproved Buffon's view that American animals were degenerate
examples: This fossil collector wrote that mastodons disproved the Comte de Buffon's view that American animals were degenerate

examples: As President, he secured the purchase of the Louisiana Territory
is better known for the Louisiana Purchase and for writing the Declaration of Independence

clues.clue: During impeached judge John Pickering's trial, Pickering challenged sitting president Jefferson to a duel
examples: During his impeachment trial, Pickering challenged this sitting president to a duel

works.name: Architecture: Monticello, UVA & the Virginia State Capitol
works.description: As an amateur Palladian architect, Jefferson designed his own mansion Monticello (with its octagonal dome inspired by the Hotel de Salm in Paris and a one-handed exterior clock), his retreat Poplar Forest, the Virginia State Capitol (modeled on the Roman temple Maison Carree with Charles-Louis Clerisseau), and the Lawn, pavilions, and Rotunda of the University of Virginia.

clues.clue: Collaborated with Charles-Louis Clerisseau to design the Virginia State Capitol, replicating the Maison Carree
examples: This architect collaborated with Charles-Louis Clerisseau to design a building that replicated the Maison Carree

clues.clue: Designed Monticello with an octagonal, skylight-studded dome influenced by the Hotel de Salm, which he visited while serving in Paris
examples: Another of his designs was influence by the Hotel de Salm, which he visited during his time in Paris

clues.clue: Monticello's entrance hall has a great clock with an exterior face that has only one hand
examples: Another of his works contains a great clock in the entrance hall, which has an exterior face with only one hand

clues.clue: Eschewed grand staircases in favor of suppressed (hidden) ones in many of his designs
examples: Many of this man's designs eschewed grand staircases for suppressed ones

clues.clue: Also designed Poplar Forest, which included extra slave quarters along Mulberry Row
examples: One building designed by this architect of Poplar Forest contained a series of extra quarters at Mulberry Row

clues.clue: Designed several buildings at the University of Virginia, including the Lawn's pavilions and the central Rotunda, as well as his own mansion Monticello
examples: name this architect who designed several buildings at the University of Virginia, as well as his own mansion, Monticello

works.name: Sally Hemings & Slavery at Monticello
works.description: Jefferson enslaved several hundred people at Monticello. Journalist James Callender, after being denied a postmaster appointment, publicized the allegation that Jefferson had fathered children with the enslaved Sally Hemings; Jefferson was also accused of hypocrisy for owning slaves in a letter from Benjamin Banneker. William Wells Brown's novel Clotel later fictionalized a daughter born of this relationship.

clues.clue: James Callender, after being denied a postmaster appointment, exposed his affair with the enslaved woman Sally Hemings
examples: James Callender published rumors that this president had kids with Sally Hemings

After getting out of jail, Callender asked this president to appoint him postmaster of Richmond, Virginia. When Callender didn't get the job, he exposed this president's affair with the enslaved woman Sally Hemings

clues.clue: Accused of hypocrisy for owning slaves in a letter from Benjamin Banneker, who also sent him an almanac urging recognition of Black equality

clues.clue: Fictionalized as the father of an enslaved half-Black daughter, Clotel, in William Wells Brown's novel, based on his real relationship with Sally Hemings
examples: This subject of Jon Meacham's biography The Art of Power is the title character's father in William Wells Brown's novel Clotel
William Wells Brown's novel Clotel; or, The President's Daughter fictionalized the life of this man's daughter by his slave Sally Hemings

clues.clue: Ibram X. Kendi's Stamped from the Beginning titles a section for him, saying he "rested in the comfort of slavery" from earliest childhood until his "final lucid moment"
examples: The second section of Ibrahim Kendi's Stamped from the Beginning is titled for this person, who Kendi says "rested in the comfort of slavery" from "his earliest childhood" until his "final lucid moment"

works.name: Friendship & Rivalry with John Adams
works.description: Jefferson and his former rival John Adams, the second and third presidents, reconciled after Benjamin Rush mediated their estrangement, then exchanged heavily studied letters for fifteen years. Both men died within hours of each other on July 4, 1826, the fiftieth anniversary of the Declaration of Independence.

clues.clue: He and Adams died within hours of each other on July 4, 1826; one's last words claimed the other survived him

clues.clue: In a letter to the other, one claimed "the same political parties which now agitate the U.S. have existed through all time"

clues.clue: In a letter to the other, blamed "Christian Priests, Jewish Rabbis, Grecian Sages and Roman Emperors" for the loss of history of nations more ancient than Christ

works.name: Legacy, Words & Biography
works.description: Beyond politics, Jefferson coined and introduced several English words, wrote a still-influential parliamentary procedure manual, and sold his personal library to found the modern Library of Congress. He has been the subject of numerous biographies examining his contradictions.

clues.clue: Coined the word "neologize" and introduced "catenary" (in a letter about bridge construction) into English
examples: Aptly, "neologize" is one of the many words coined by this person, who introduced the word "catenary" in a letter about bridge construction written to a friend

clues.clue: Wrote a Manual of Parliamentary Practice outlining procedures for dealing with "disorderly words," while serving as Vice President
examples: Procedures for dealing with "disorderly words" are outlined in this person's Manual of Parliamentary Practice

clues.clue: Sold his personal book collection to the Library of Congress in 1815, replacing the collection destroyed during the War of 1812
examples: The Library of Congress received many of its books in 1815 in order to replace the collection destroyed in the War of 1812. Those books came from the voluminous personal collection of this former president
This place's owner settled some of his extensive debt by selling off its book collection to form the Library of Congress

clues.clue: In 1802, the townspeople of Chester, Massachusetts presented him with a 1,235-pound "Mammoth Cheese" inscribed "Rebellion to Tyrants is Obedience to God"
examples: In 1802, the people of Chester, Massachusetts produced a "Mammoth Cheese" inscribed with the motto "Rebellion to Tyrants is Obedience to God" for this 3rd U.S. President who took two years to get through it

clues.clue: Examined in Joseph Ellis' biography American Sphinx as a "feminine" and "dinner table" politician who hid from the public; his 158 letters with his predecessor (John Adams) were studied in Jon Meacham's The Art of Power
examples: Joseph Ellis argued this politician was a "feminine" and "dinner table" politician who hid from the public in his biography American Sphinx. 158 letters this politician exchanged with his predecessor were examined by Jon Meacham as exemplifying his "aging revolutionary" character in The Art of Power

clues.clue: The first comprehensive biography of him was written by early science-fiction author and Congressman George Tucker
examples: The first comprehensive biography of this politician was written by early science fiction author and Congressman George Tucker

clues.clue: In his conservative later years, novelist John Dos Passos wrote a study of his "head and heart"
examples: In his conservative later years, Dos Passos wrote a study of the "head and heart" of this man, whose own writings include Notes on the State of Virginia

comprehensive_summary: Thomas Jefferson served as the first US Secretary of State under George Washington, the second US Minister to France, and the third President of the United States, having earlier drafted the Declaration of Independence as part of the Committee of Five; his political thought distinguished a "natural aristocracy" of virtue and talent from an "artificial aristocracy" of wealth and birth, and championed an agrarian republic of yeoman farmers against Alexander Hamilton's centralized vision, later authoring the Kentucky Resolutions after resigning the vice presidency over the Alien and Sedition Acts. A committed advocate of church-state separation, he wrote the Virginia Statute for Religious Freedom and, in an 1802 letter to the Danbury Baptists, called for a "wall of separation between church and state"; his dissatisfaction with Virginia's religious institutions led him to found the University of Virginia. His deism led him to physically cut and paste passages from the New Testament with a razor and glue to isolate "the very words of Jesus," excising every miracle in what became known as the Jefferson Bible. His only published book, Notes on the State of Virginia, refuted Comte de Buffon's theory of American degeneracy with tables of quadruped sizes and the oration of Mingo chief Logan, while also containing Jefferson's contradictory and often racist views on Black Americans, including his infamous dismissal of poet Phillis Wheatley. As president, Jefferson refused continued tribute to the Pasha of Tripoli, triggering the First Barbary War before he ultimately negotiated peace and signed the Embargo Act; he secured the Louisiana Purchase and commissioned the Lewis and Clark Expedition, partly hoping it would find a living mastodon after he had used mastodon fossils to rebut Buffon. An amateur Palladian architect, he designed his mansion Monticello (with its Hotel de Salm-inspired octagonal dome and one-handed exterior clock), his retreat Poplar Forest, the Virginia State Capitol (with Charles-Louis Clerisseau, modeled on the Maison Carree), and the Lawn and Rotunda of the University of Virginia. He enslaved several hundred people at Monticello; journalist James Callender, denied a postmaster appointment, publicized Jefferson's relationship with the enslaved Sally Hemings, while Benjamin Banneker accused him of hypocrisy for owning slaves. Jefferson and his former rival John Adams reconciled through the mediation of Benjamin Rush and corresponded for fifteen years, dying within hours of each other on July 4, 1826. Beyond politics, he coined the word "neologize," introduced "catenary" and "pedicure" into English, wrote an influential Manual of Parliamentary Practice, and sold his personal library to found the modern Library of Congress after the original collection burned in the War of 1812.
recursive_suggestions: Monticello — appeared as its own answerline (2024 History of the World Part II, 2023 Chicago Open); rich standalone detail on its enslaved community, architecture, and the Getting Word project
Thomas Jefferson-Sally Hemings controversy — appeared as its own answerline (2014 VCU Open); detailed coverage of the DNA evidence, Callender's role, and the 2001 Scholars' Commission
Slaves of Thomas Jefferson — appeared as its own answerline (2014 College History Bowl); covers named individuals (Peter Fossett, the Hemings family) and Monticello's nail factory
Meriwether Lewis — appeared as its own answerline (2026 ACF Nationals); leader of the Lewis and Clark Expedition Jefferson commissioned
John Adams — recurring co-figure with enough independent detail (correspondence, presidency) for his own page
Notes on the State of Virginia — appeared as its own answerline (bonus part); Jefferson's only book, with detailed content on Buffon's refutation and race
The Life and Morals of Jesus of Nazareth (The Jefferson Bible) — appeared as its own answerline (2024 JaMES); detailed textual/compositional history
